mapping.a_element: recurrent neural network as a learning system that must compress its input
mapping.b_element: bounded consumer/agent modeled as a limited information channel

mapping.a_element: explicit information bottleneck (rate-limited compression) applied to the network
mapping.b_element: finite attention/channel capacity that compresses the agent's world

mapping.a_element: high-dimensional neural activity / time-series environment being compressed
mapping.b_element: the full 'world' of feasible baskets the shopper faces

mapping.a_element: low-dimensional learned representation the network acts on
mapping.b_element: compressed, stochastic choice (a probability distribution over baskets)

mapping.a_element: bottleneck tightness / capacity as the control parameter (scaling with task complexity)
mapping.b_element: attention capacity as the control parameter interpolating the agent
unlimited-attention limit recovering the exact Walrasian/textbook consumer

mapping.a_element: compression itself (not neuron-level activity) causally producing the functional advantage of generalisation
mapping.b_element: compression plus the budget constraint (not rationality) causally producing Slutsky symmetry and downward-sloping demand

brief_justification: Both instantiate the same information-bottleneck / finite-capacity information-channel (rate-distortion) machinery: a system compresses a high-dimensional world into a capacity-limited representation, a single capacity parameter interpolates between a full-capacity limit (memorisation / the Walrasian consumer) and a degenerate low-capacity limit (pure habit), and each paper's central claim is that the emergent structured behaviour (generalisation in A; Slutsky-symmetric, downward-sloping demand in B) arises causally from the compression itself rather than from the conventional explanation.
